coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot with co-doped nitrogen and phosphorus for tazettine ratiometric fluorescent sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Kanjun
Wu, Yusheng
Yang, Juan
Wang, Na
Mo, Keting
Zeng, Chujie
Deng, Biyang
coredata.dc:description: An overdose of tazettine (TZ) has toxic side effects, which makes it extremely important to rapidly and subtly determine the TZ. In this study, double-emitting, nitrogen–phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) were prepared using cis-butenedioic acid, phosphoric acid, and p-phenylenediamine. An effective inner filter effect (IFE) can occur between TZ and N,P-CQDs, resulting in their fluorescence quench. Therefore, a ratio fluorescent probe was constructed for detecting the TZ was constructed. After optimizing the experimental conditions, the quenching efficiency showed a strong linear connection with the TZ concentration in the range of 0.01–30 µmol/L, with the detection limit of 0.002 µmol/L. This method could be satisfactorily applied to detect TZ in mouse plasma samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Tazettine
Fluorescence probe
Inner filter effect
Ratiometric fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions for N,P-CQDs
The mechanism for the optical response of N,P-CQDs to TZ

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of N,P-CQDs, (b) the size distribution of N,P-CQDs, (c) the XRD pattern of N,P-CQDs, (d) the FTIR spectrum of N,P-CQDs.
XPS spectrum of N,P-CQDs, (a) high-resolution XPS of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) P 2p.
Fluorescence spectra of N,P-CQDs synthesized at different (a) temperatures, (b) reaction times, (c) phosphoric acid contents, and (d) dosing ratios.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N,P-CQDs (5 µg/mL), inset: photos of N,P-CQDs solution under natural light irradiation (left panel) and 365-nm UV light irradiation (right panel), (b) the fluorescence spectra of N,P-CQDs (10 µg/mL), (c) the fluorescence emission spectra of N,P-CQDs (10 µg/mL) at different excitation wavelengths.
Stability of N,P-CQDs (10 µg/mL) at (a) pH ranges of 1.0–10.0, (b) NaCl concentration, (c) effect of 365-nm UV lamp irradiation time, (d) effect of the storage time (error bar = SD, n = 3).

(a) Effect of pH on F0/F (N,P-CQDs: 10 µg/mL; TZ: 30 µmol/L; buffer solution: pH 4 phosphate buffer; error bar = SD, n = 3) (b) Effect of reaction time on F331/F481 values (N,P-CQDs: 10 µg/mL; TZ: 30 µmol/L; buffer solution: pH 4 phosphate buffer; error bar = SD, n = 3).

(a) Fluorescence response of N,P-CQDs (10 µg/mL) after the addition of different concentrations of TZ and (b) linear relationship between TZ concentration and F331/F481 values (N,P-CQDs: 10 µg/mL; error bar = SD, n = 3).

Effect of possible interfering substances on the F331/F481 ratio (N,P-CQDs: 10 µg/mL, each substance: 30 µmol/L, PBS: pH = 4; error bar = SD, n = 3). (A: Blank, B: GSH, C: ASA, D: BPF, E: Urea, F: Gly, G: Glucose, H: L-Cys, I: Na+, J: Mg2+, K: Al3+, L: Zn2+, M: Hg2+, N: Cu2+, O: Fe2+, P: Ca2+, Q: Ag+, R: MnO4 −, S: CO3 –, T: HSO3 −, U: HCO3 –, V: SO4 2−, W: PO4 3−, X: TZ).

(a) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (b) Lifetime of N,P-CQDs in the absence (black) and presence (red) of 50 μM TZ. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Linear relationship between TZ concentration and F0/F at different temperatures.
Preparation of N,P-CQDs and the detection of the TZ.
Comparison of different methods for the determination of TZ.
The detection results and recovery of TZ in the mouse plasma samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot with co-doped nitrogen and phosphorus for tazettine ratiometric fluorescent sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Lab of Agricultural Resources Chemistry and Biotechnology, School of Chemistry and Food Science, Yulin Normal University, Yulin 537000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Lab of Agricultural Resources Chemistry and Biotechnology
School of Chemistry and Food Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Lab of Agricultural Resources Chemistry and Biotechnology, School of Chemistry and Food Science, Yulin Normal University, Yulin 537000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Kanjun Fang and Yusheng Wu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An overdose of tazettine (TZ) has toxic side effects, which makes it extremely important to rapidly and subtly determine the TZ. In this study, double-emitting, nitrogen–phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) were prepared using cis-butenedioic acid, phosphoric acid, and p-phenylenediamine. An effective inner filter effect (IFE) can occur between TZ and N,P-CQDs, resulting in their fluorescence quench. Therefore, a ratio fluorescent probe was constructed for detecting the TZ was constructed. After optimizing the experimental conditions, the quenching efficiency showed a strong linear connection with the TZ concentration in the range of 0.01–30 µmol/L, with the detection limit of 0.002 µmol/L. This method could be satisfactorily applied to detect TZ in mouse plasma samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tazettine (TZ), an alkaloid produced by plants of the Lithospermum genus, can be isolated from Narcissus multiflora, and it is produced by the rearrangement of the unstable parent alkaloid pretazettine during alkaloid extraction [1–3]. TZ is clinically used to treat cough, toothache, sore throat, and bruises. However, the minimum toxic dose is 20 μg/mL [4], which is close to the therapeutic dose. Therefore, rapidly and sensitively determining the TZ is critical to avoid the development of toxic side effects. The current methods used to detect TZ include capillary electrophoresis (CE) [5], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [6], and reverse high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7]. However, these methods are typically time-consuming, expensive, and non-portable, have limited sensitivity, and necessitate specialized technicians and complex equipment. Thus, there is a critical need to develop a simple, rapid, and economical fluorescence spectrometric technique for determining the TZ.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a relatively newer type of carbon nanomaterials with a particle size of <10 nm [8]. CQDs have stable physicochemical characteristics and are one of the most widely studied carbon nanomaterials. The advantages of CQDs include environmental safety, poor bioavailability, low cost, and significant biocompatibility, which makes them ideal raw materials for building sensing devices [9–11]. In recent years, CQDs have been widely used in drug delivery [12–14], bioimaging [15–17], and fluorescence sensing [18–20]. CQDs can be prepared by various techniques, among which the hydrothermal method is the simplest and the most environmentally friendly one [21]. Several studies have been performed to enhance the efficiency of CQDs for achieving good tunable properties [22–24]. Among these, heteroatom doping is used to modify the surface characteristics and fluorescence properties of CQDs [25–26]. Doping with S, N, F, P, and B has been demonstrated to effectively change the electronic structure and optoelectronic properties of CQDs, thereby improving the quantum yield and extending their practical applications [27–29]. The ratio fluorescence probes are used to improve detection sensitivity by simultaneously measuring the fluorescence intensity of different emission peaks and calculating their intensity ratios, thereby effectively reducing instrument and background interference through self-calibration. This detection method is more accurate than intensity-based fluorescence probes [30–32] and has been used in the pharmaceutical [33] and food industries [34].

In this study, a simple hydrothermal strategy was applied to prepare double-emitting N,P-CQDs using cis-butenedioic acid, phosphoric acid, and p-phenylenediamine as precursors. Scheme 1 depicts the N,P-CQD synthesis pathway as well as the detection principle of TZ. This method displayed the superiority of N,P-CQDs for TZ detection and was used to detect TZ in mouse plasma samples.

Maleic acid (HPLC), p-phenylenediamine (PPA), and phosphoric acid were procured from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). TZ was procured from Baichuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Xi’an, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was procured from PerkinElmer Co., Ltd. (USA).

The following apparatuses were used in this study: the RF-5301PC fluorescent spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), Cary-60 UV–vis spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), FL3-P-TCSPC time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, France), FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK), Spectrum Two Fourier-transformed infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA), and D/max 2500/PC powder X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan).

To 30 mL of ultrapure water, 0.24 g of cis-butenedioic acid, 0.16 g of p-phenylenediamine, and 2 mL of phosphoric acid were added. Then, the mixture was heated in a Teflon autoclave (50 mL) at 230 °C for 6 h. After the reactor cooled naturally, the mixture was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, dialysis was performed after filtering the mixture through a 0.22-µm membrane.

The absolute FLQY was measured in an integrating sphere with excitation of 290 nm and ultrapure water as the blank solution using an Edinburgh FLS1000 full-featured steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer.

N,P-CQDs solution (10 µL) and 20 µL of different concentrations of TZ were mixed and the volume was adjusted to 500 µL with PBS buffer (pH 4.0). The fluorescence intensities of 331 nm and 481 nm of the samples were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 290 nm.

BALB/c female mice (age: 6–8 weeks) were purchased from Hunan SJA Laboratory Animal (Changsha, China). Animal treatment methods were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of the Guangxi Normal University (GXNU-202303–051), and the mice were kept under specific pathogen-free (SPF) conditions with free access to standard food and water. All animal studies were carried out strictly under the guidelines of the National Regulations on the Care and Use of Experimental Animals of China.

The mice were fasted one day before the experiment (except for water). The next day, the experimental mice were administered 2.0 mg/kg body weight intravenously. Two hours after injection, the blood of the mice was extracted into centrifuge tubes containing sodium heparin. The plasma was treated as described previously [35]. Briefly, the blood sample was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 15 min. The drug was extracted from 400 μL of plasma by passing 1 mL of ethyl acetate and 100 μL of 0.1 mol/L sodium hydroxide. The mixture was vortexed for 2 min and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was decanted into a test tube. The pellet was extracted twice using the same procedure. The extract was then placed in a water bath at 80 °C and desolvated with nitrogen. The residue was dissolved in 400 μL of the buffer solution and kept for TZ measurement.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed to observe the surface characteristics and structure of N,P-CQDs. As seen in Fig. 1 a, they exhibit a near-spherical shape, a graphitic crystal structure, and good dispersion, and the lattice spacing is 0.227 nm, indicating a high degree of graphitization [36]. The particle size ranges from 1.22 to 4.38 nm and the average particle size is 2.62 nm (Fig. 1b). There is a strong peak at 2θ = 23.6° in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1c), further demonstrating that they have an amorphous carbon structure [37]. To explore the composition of the functional groups, FTIR characterization was performed (Fig. 1d). The characteristic bands at 3436 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H. In addition, the peak at 1403 cm−1 may be due to the C-N bond, whereas the peak at 1185 cm−1 may be caused by the C-O stretching vibration. These results indicate that the N,P elements are doped into N,P-CQDs and the surface has some groups that lead to high water solubility and excellent fluorescence properties.

XPS analysis revealed that the N,P-CQDs were primarily composed of four elements, C, N, O, and P, with atomic ratios of 47.84%, 4.56%, 15.72%, and 2.23% (Fig. 2 a), respectively. Four peaks were visible in the XPS full spectrum of N,P-CQDs. They were at 284.17 eV, 399.35 eV, 531.29 eV, and 132.93 eV and attributed to the signal peaks of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and P 2p. The high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 2b) contained four peaksat 284.40 eV, 284.88 eV, 285.95 eV, and 288.54 eV. They are attributed to CC, CC, CO/CN, and OC/CN.The high-resolution N 1s spectra exhibited two peaks, 399.06 eV and 400.14 eV, respectively, which were attributed to CNC and NC3 (Fig. 2c). The high-resolution O 1s spectra contained two peaks at 530.18 eV and 531.68 eV (Fig. 2d), which were due to the CO and OCO functional groups, respectively. The high-resolution P 2p spectra (Fig. 2e) contained two peaks at 132.64 eV and 133.53 eV. Among them, 132.64 eV indicated the presence of PO and 133.53 eV was attributed to PO [38]. These results indicate that the elements N and P were doped onto the CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions for N,P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain N,P-CQDs with good fluorescence performance, the effects of phosphoric acid dosage, the ingredient ratio on the synthesis reaction, reaction temperature, and time were investigated. Maximum fluorescence intensity at 331 nm was observed when the temperature was 230 °C (Fig. 3 a), the reaction time was 6 h (Fig. 3b), 2 mL of phosphoric acid was used (Fig. 3c), and the ratio of cis- and p-phenylenediamine was 3:2 (Fig. 3d).

N,P-CQDs appear light brown in the natural light (Fig. 4 a, inset) and fluoresce light green when they are irradiated with UV light at 365 nm. The UV absorption spectra (Fig. 4a) showed absorption peaks at 268 nm and 382 nm, of which the peak at 268 nm may be attributed to the π-π* leap of CC [39–40]. As shown in the fluorescence spectrogram in Fig. 4b, the emission peaks exhibited an excitation wavelength-independent property. The fluorescence spectrum revealed two emission peaks at 331 nm and 481 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength of the emission peak at 331 nm for N,P-CQDs was 290 nm, and the intensity of the emission peak at 331 nm first increased and then decreased under different excitation wavelengths (260–310 nm) (Fig. 4c). Since the emission peak at 331 nm exhibited stronger sensitivity to TZ, the excitation wavelength for the test was set to 290 nm. The absolute FLQY of N,P-CQDs, as determined by an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer, was 1.69%.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, under acidic conditions, N,P-CQDs were significantly more stable at pH 1–6. The fluorescence intensity remained unchanged when the concentration of NaCl was increased (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence intensity decreased slightly after exposure to continuous irradiation by using a UV lamp for 30 min (Fig. 5c), indicating that N,P-CQDs exhibit good anti-photobleaching properties. Furthermore, after 1 month of storage at 4 °C, the intensity did not decrease significantly (Fig. 5d). These findings confirm excellent stability of N,P-CQDs.

To determine the optimal conditions for detecting TZ, the effects of reaction time and pH of the buffer were examined. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the maximum value of F0/F was obtained when the pH was 4. F0/F indicates the proportion of the intensity at 331 nm before and after the addition of TZ. As shown in Fig. 6b, when the reaction time reached 1 min, F331/F481 no longer changed, demonstrating that the reaction of TZ and N,P-CQDs was both fast and stable within 1 min. According to these results, the pH value of the PBS buffer was selected as 4 for the experiment and the optimal reaction time for detecting TZ was 1 min.

We investigated the effect of the possible interferences to confirm the viability of N,P-CQDs for detecting TZ in real samples (Fig. 8 ). These substances include acetylsalicylic acid, ibuprofen, urea, gly, glucose, L-cysteine, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Ag+, MnO4 −, CO3 2–, HSO3 −, HCO3 –, SO4 2−, and PO4 3−. The results indicated that these substances did not affect F331/F481. According to our results, N,P-CQDs exhibited good selectivity for TZ as a ratiometric fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for the optical response of N,P-CQDs to TZ
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the fluorescence quench mechanism of N,P-CQDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence lifetime were characterized. As shown in Fig. 9 a, by comparing the absorption spectra of a mixture of N,P-CQDs and TZ with the fitted curve, the absorbance perfectly matched, indicating that there is no chemical reaction between N,P-CQDs and TZ. Furthermore, TZ showed a strong absorption peak between 300 and 400 nm, which overlapped with the first emission peak of N,P-CQDs, indicating that the quenching mechanism could be an inner filter effect (IFE) or resonance energy transfer. The key to distinguishing them is based on whether the fluorescence lifetime changes. As shown in Fig. 9b, the average fluorescence lifetime of N,P-CQDs was 1.25 ns, and 1.23 ns after TZ was added. The fluorescence lifetime barely changed after adding TZ, indicating that fluorescence quenching followed a static quenching process and IFE. According to these findings, fluorescence quenching was caused by the static quenching effect and IFE. To further distinguish the quenching mechanism from static quenching or dynamic quenching, the relationship between TZ concentration and F0/F at different temperatures was characterized. According to the Stern–Volmer equation (eq1), F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher, respectively. KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher. As shown in Fig. 10 , as the temperature increased, the Ksv constant decreased, indicating that the quenching mechanism is static quenching, and the results are consistent with the fluorescence lifetime characterization. According to these examinations, fluorescence quenching was caused by the static quenching effect and IFE.
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]

To confirm the practicality of the prepared fluorescent probes for detecting TZ in real complex samples, they were used for detecting TZ in the mouse plasma samples. The concentration of TZ in the mouse plasma samples was determined by the standard addition methods. TZ was added to the mouse plasma samples in three concentrations: 5.000, 10.00, and 20.00 μmol/L. The recoveries of TZ in plasma samples ranged from 97.46% to 103.0% with relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 5) of <3.1%. Table 2 summarizes that the strategy was accurate, reproducible, and successful in detecting TZ in real samples.

In summary, a novel fluorescent sensor based on green N,P-CQDs was successfully synthesized. The method is simple and cost-effective for detecting TZ in the mouse plasma samples. IFE causes its fluorescence quenching, and the proportional fluorescent probe was constructed because of the different degrees of quenching of the two emission peaks to improve its anti-interference ability and stability. The method exhibits excellent selectivity and low detection limits in complex environments.

Kanjun Fang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Juan Yang: Writing-review & editing. Na Wang: Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Keting Mo: Writing - review & editing. Chujie Zeng: Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition Biyang Deng: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22164004, 21964019) and the Guangxi Science Foundation of China (2022GXNSFDA035069).
